source_metadata.title_raw: Michael J. Maurer - A-19-104 - June 26, 2019 - Los Angeles

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael J. Maurer 
Best Best & Krieger 
300 South Grande Ave, 25th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90071 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-104 
Dear Mr. Maurer: 
June 26, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
May Jessica Grinberg, a member of the Mendocino Coast District ("District") Board of 
Directors ("Board") take part in the Board's decision on whether to have the Mendocino 
Coast District Hospital ("Hospital") affiliate with and be operated by another medical 
service provider, given that she is currently the sole provider of orthotics and prosthetics 
medical services in the community, and has a contract with the Hospital? 
2. 
May Karen Arnold, also a member of the Board, take part in the above decision, given that 
she works for a local nonprofit medical clinic that competes with Hospital, as well as 
· another medical service provider that could potentially submit a proposal for affiliation, in 
the realm of health care services? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-19-104 
Page No. 2 
1. 
No, because the decision of whether Hospital will affiliate with and be operated by another 
medical services provider would present a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect 
on Director Grinberg's personal finances and economic interest in her business. 
2. 
No, because the decision would present a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect 
on the local clinic, 'in which Director Arnold has a source of income interest. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
The Board will be considering whether to affiliate with a private operator for operation of 
Hospital (located in Fort Bragg), which is currently owned and operated by the District. 
Director Jessica Grinberg 
Jessica Grinberg is a member of the Board. Sht; is also the sole owner of a local orthotics 
and prosthetics company ("Company") that, in addition to a clinical practice as a primary source of 
income, provides equipment to patients at Hospital and at a single Adventist hospital in a nearb_y 
town. 
Company has two contracts with Hospital and the Adventist hospital to use its products at 
the hospital. The contracts contain essentially identical terms and are both non-exclusive, such that 
other competing companies can have similar contracts with the same hospitals. Currently, there are 
no competing local businesses. Additionally, Hospital is in an isolated location. Director Grinberg 
is the only orthotics and prosthetics provider within a two-hour drive of the community. Company's 
referrals come from doctors within the local medical community. Director Grinberg expects that 
any group that Hospital would potentially affiliate with would end up using her services, even if the 
doctors are not from the area, although most likely the doctors will be the current local doctors. In 
her words, her work is "hands on" and "not done remotely." She believes it would most likely be 
. cost prohibitive to expect another practitioner to travel to the area. 
Director Karen Arnold 
Karen Arnold is also a member of the Board and is employed by and receives income from a 
federally qualified'health medical clinic in Fort Bragg, Mendocino Coast Clinics ("Clinic"). Clinic 
is a non-profit entity that offers general family health services, but also provides women's health, 
behavioral health, pediatrics, and dental services. Hospital currently owns and operates the North 
Coast Family Health Center ("Health Center"), which is a smaller family practice clinic. Adventist 
also currently operates a very small rural health center in the same area as Health Center. 
Adventist's rural health center offers family practice services for both adult and pediatrics, but does 
not offer specialized services for women's health, behavioral health, or dental care. Clinic is a 
larger practice, but there are likely some patients who utilize Clinic's services that could instead use 
Adventist or Health Center. To some extent, all three entities currently compete for the same 
patients. 


File No. A-19-104 
Page No. 3 
Per your request for advice, there has been "some speculation" that if Hospital were to 
affiliate with Adventist, a potential proposer, then Adventist might change the operations of its rural 
health clinic to more directly compete with Clinic. Adventist could potentially combine Health 
Center with its rural health center to build a larger practice. To your knowledge, however, .this 
scenario is based on speculation, not any current proposal by Adventist. The operation of 
Adventist's private clinic is not, and would not be, subject to District oversight or supervision. If 
Hospital were to affiliate with Adventist, then Adventist would operate the District's services, but 
that affiliation and operation would not create additional competition in the local health care 
market. 
Hospital Request for Proposals 
The Hospital Request for Proposals ("RFP") indicates that the Board is interested in 
identifying a parfner with specific characteristics that include "[ s ]ufficient capital to allow 
[Hospital] to maintain high-quality care for its patients and improve its physical facilities." The RFP 
asks that proposers to "[ d]escribe the organizational structure or structures which your business or 
businesses are conducted (including joint ventures and partnerships) and the current scope of 
services you provide." Proposers are also asked to agree to several "Commitments," including 
"[r]etention of current [Hospital] staff, who_ are currently under the employment of the current 
lessee of the Hospital," and are instructed to "[d]escribe your commitment to retain [Hospital] 
employees following the closing of a transaction." With regard to "[ c ]linical services and physician 
recruiting," proposers are also instructed to: 
• 
Describe your.commitment to take steps to ensure that, subject to patient choice, all medical 
services for which there exists financial capability at [Hospital] will be performed locally 
rather than at outlying, tertiary care facilities, whether or not owned or controlled by your 
organization. 
• 
I;)escribe your commitment to maintaining existing clinical services at [Hospital] and 
indicate the minimum timing that you are prepared to make for maintaining such services. 
• 
Describe any new services that you anticipate implementing during the next five years. 
• 
Describe commitment to expend significant financial resources to recruit new physicians to 
the community and grow [Hospital]'s current complement of clinical services, and specify 
what level of capital commitment you are making to do so. 
• 
Describe which, if any, clinical services may not be maintained at [Hospital]. 
During a phone call, you stated that neither Director Grinberg, nor Director Arnold, had any 
involvement in the production or issuance of the RFP. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
Under Section 87100 of the Act, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 


FileNo. A-19-104 
Page No. 4 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) The financial interests implicated by 
your facts include: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or 
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
Foreseeapility 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 provides that "[a] financial effect ... is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency." None of the Directors' interests are explicitly 
involved in the decisions in question. Thus, under Regulation 18701(b). Where an interest is not 
explicitly involved in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered foreseeable. 
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, then it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be 
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, then it is 
not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality 
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a source of income is material if the source is a 
nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) 
Under Regulation 18702.S(a), "[ a] personal financial effect means the financial effect of a 
governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive 
a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." 
Under Regulation 18702.1 (b ), where a business entity interest is not expressly involved in a 
governmental decision, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the business is still considered 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately held business 
entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable include those that increase or decrease the 
amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged, or increase or decrease 


File No. A-19-104 
Page No. 5 
the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies. (Regulation 18702.1 (b )(2)-
(3).) 
Lastly, under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), for income received by an official for goods and 
services provided in the ordinary course of business, including a salary, a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect is material if the source of income is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable 
financial benefit or loss, or the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has an 
interest in real property that will be financially affected under the standards applied to a financial 
interest in Regulation 18702.2 ( containing the materiality standard for real property interests). 
Director Grinberg 
Director Grinberg is currently the only orthotics and prosthetics provider in the local 
community. The next closest provider is approximately two hours away. She has described her 
work as "hands on" and "not done remotely." Director Grinberg expects that "any group that comes 
in will end up using [her] services, even if the doctors are not from the area (although most likely 
the doctors will be the current locals)," and she believes, "[i]t is most likely cost prohibitive to 
expect another practitioner to travel to the area." Her contracts with both Hospital and Adventist are 
non-exclusive, so other competing companies could contract with those hospitals. 
You have not provided any facts indicating that any proposals have been received. However, 
the content of the RFP supports the conclusion that the Board anticipates having to make a decision 
on a potential affiliate based, at least in part, on the services they would provide, whether they 
would maintain current staff/employees (many of whom have a pre-existing relationship with 
Director Grinberg), and additional changes they might make, including implementing new services 
and upgrading the hospital. 
Based on the facts provided, there appears to be a realistic possibility and "more than 
hypothetical or theoretical," that the Board of Directors' decision to affiliate with another medical 
service provider could affect Director Grinberg's personal finances or her business entity interest. 
For instance, if one medical service provider would potentially provide some of the products and 
services that Director Grinberg currently provides, she would suffer a measurable financial loss 
from that change in circumstances. (See Regulation 18702.S(a).) Likewise, the decision would 
potentially increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field or the need for the products 
and services that Director Grinberg provides. (See Regulation 18702.l(b)(2)-(3).) Thus, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value 
of Director Grinberg's company. The reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Director Grinberg's 
company is, therefore, material under Regulation 18702.l(b). Similarly, because Director Grinberg 
owns that company, a material effect on her company would presumably result in a measurable 
financial benefit or loss to her as an individual, thereby constituting a material financial effect on 
Director Grinberg's personal finances under Regulation 18702.S(a).) For these reasons, she is 
prohibited from taking part in the proposal process, including the final decision of whether to 
affiliate with another medical service provider. (See Regulation 18707 .) 


Director Arnold 
File No. A-19-104 
Page No. 6 
Director Arnold has a source of income interest in her non-profit employer, Clinic. 
Assuming Clinic does not submit a proposal to affiliate and operate Hospital, the governmental 
decision before Director Arnold will not explicitly involve her economic interest. (See Regulation 
18701(a).) However, it is reasonably foreseeable that if another medical care provider were to 
affiliate with and operate Hospital (as well as Health Center, which is owned and operated by 
Hospital), Clinic could be financially affected. (See Regulation 18701 (b ). ) Where a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss for a non-profit 
organization, the financial effect is deemed "material." (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) A new 
affiliate/operator could be a more desirable medical care provider than Clinic for several reasons, 
such as being part of a larger network, providing less expensive goods and services, or providing a 
wider range of services. Even if Hospital's transfer of responsibility and authority for operating the 
hospital did not technically create "more competition" in the same way that the construction of a 
new hospital would, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision could affect the current state of 
competition and result in a measurable financial benefit or loss for Clinic. (Regulations 18701(b).) 
As above, this conclusion is supported by the content of the RFP, which anticipates potential 
changes in services provided and upgrades to Hospital. Accordingly, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect is material and Director Arnold is disqualified from taking part in the proposal 
process, including the decision of whether to have Hospital affiliate with and be operated by 
another health care provider. (See Regulation 18707.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Counsel, Legal Division

2.

on the local clinic, 'in which Director Arnold has a source of income interest.

sections.analysis:
The Act 
Under Section 87100 of the Act, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 


FileNo. A-19-104  
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) The financial interests implicated by 
your facts include: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or 
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
Foreseeapility 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 provides that "[a] financial effect ... is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency." None of the Directors' interests are explicitly 
involved in the decisions in question. Thus, under Regulation 18701(b). Where an interest is not 
explicitly involved in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered foreseeable. 
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, then it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be 
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, then it is 
not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality 
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a source of income is material if the source is a 
nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) 
Under Regulation 18702.S(a), "[ a] personal financial effect means the financial effect of a 
governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive 
a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." 
Under Regulation 18702.1 (b ), where a business entity interest is not expressly involved in a 
governmental decision, a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the business is still considered 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately held business 
entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable include those that increase or decrease the 
amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged, or increase or decrease  
the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies. (Regulation 18702.1 (b )(2)-
(3).) 
Lastly, under Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), for income received by an official for goods and 
services provided in the ordinary course of business, including a salary, a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect is material if the source of income is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable 
financial benefit or loss, or the official knows or has reason to know that the nonprofit has an 
interest in real property that will be financially affected under the standards applied to a financial 
interest in Regulation 18702.2 ( containing the materiality standard for real property interests). 
Director Grinberg 
Director Grinberg is currently the only orthotics and prosthetics provider in the local 
community. The next closest provider is approximately two hours away. She has described her 
work as "hands on" and "not done remotely." Director Grinberg expects that "any group that comes 
in will end up using [her] services, even if the doctors are not from the area (although most likely 
the doctors will be the current locals)," and she believes, "[i]t is most likely cost prohibitive to 
expect another practitioner to travel to the area." Her contracts with both Hospital and Adventist are 
non-exclusive, so other competing companies could contract with those hospitals. 
You have not provided any facts indicating that any proposals have been received. However, 
the content of the RFP supports the conclusion that the Board anticipates having to make a decision 
on a potential affiliate based, at least in part, on the services they would provide, whether they 
would maintain current staff/employees (many of whom have a pre-existing relationship with 
Director Grinberg), and additional changes they might make, including implementing new services 
and upgrading the hospital. 
Based on the facts provided, there appears to be a realistic possibility and "more than 
hypothetical or theoretical," that the Board of Directors' decision to affiliate with another medical 
service provider could affect Director Grinberg's personal finances or her business entity interest. 
For instance, if one medical service provider would potentially provide some of the products and 
services that Director Grinberg currently provides, she would suffer a measurable financial loss 
from that change in circumstances. (See Regulation 18702.S(a).) Likewise, the decision would 
potentially increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field or the need for the products 
and services that Director Grinberg provides. (See Regulation 18702.l(b)(2)-(3).) Thus, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value 
of Director Grinberg's company. The reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Director Grinberg's 
company is, therefore, material under Regulation 18702.l(b). Similarly, because Director Grinberg 
owns that company, a material effect on her company would presumably result in a measurable 
financial benefit or loss to her as an individual, thereby constituting a material financial effect on 
Director Grinberg's personal finances under Regulation 18702.S(a).) For these reasons, she is 
prohibited from taking part in the proposal process, including the final decision of whether to 
affiliate with another medical service provider. (See Regulation 18707 .) 


Director Arnold  
Director Arnold has a source of income interest in her non-profit employer, Clinic. 
Assuming Clinic does not submit a proposal to affiliate and operate Hospital, the governmental 
decision before Director Arnold will not explicitly involve her economic interest. (See Regulation 
18701(a).) However, it is reasonably foreseeable that if another medical care provider were to 
affiliate with and operate Hospital (as well as Health Center, which is owned and operated by 
Hospital), Clinic could be financially affected. (See Regulation 18701 (b ). ) Where a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss for a non-profit 
organization, the financial effect is deemed "material." (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) A new 
affiliate/operator could be a more desirable medical care provider than Clinic for several reasons, 
such as being part of a larger network, providing less expensive goods and services, or providing a 
wider range of services. Even if Hospital's transfer of responsibility and authority for operating the 
hospital did not technically create "more competition" in the same way that the construction of a 
new hospital would, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision could affect the current state of

As above, this conclusion is supported by the content of the RFP, which anticipates potential 
changes in services provided and upgrades to Hospital. Accordingly, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect is material and Director Arnold is disqualified from taking part in the proposal 
process, including the decision of whether to have Hospital affiliate with and be operated by 
another health care provider. (See Regulation 18707.)

the realm of health care services?

on Director Grinberg's personal finances and economic interest in her business.

on the local clinic, 'in which Director Arnold has a source of income interest.
